patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series simplifies the control flow in mm functions by replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup using spinlock guards. The patches are ordered to minimize churn and have been rebased on mm-stable.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Prefers to wait until after the -rc1 merge window to consider new code changes, but encourages discussion and feedback on the use of spinlock guards in mm functions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 27 Mar 2026 16:14:40 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> This series uses spinlock guard for zone lock across several mm
> functions to replace explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic
> scope-based cleanup.
> 
> This simplifies the control flow by removing 'flags' variables, goto
> labels, and redundant unlock calls.
> 
> Patches are ordered by decreasing value. The first six patches simplify
> the control flow by removing gotos, multiple unlock paths, or 'ret'
> variables. The last two are simpler lock/unlock pair conversions that
> only remove 'flags' and can be dropped if considered unnecessary churn.

Thanks, you've been busy.

I'm not wanting to move new, non-fix, non-speedup things into mm.git
until after -rc1 so there's your target.  But now is a good time to be
sending out material for people to look at.  Let's not have a gigantic
flood of new stuff the day after -rc1!

I think progress here is totally dependent on whether those who
regularly work on this code want guard() in there.  A
preference/familiarity/style choice, mainly.  At present the adoption
of guard() in mm/*.c is very small.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 01/16] nfsdctl: move *_netlink.h to support/include/
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the *_netlink.h header files from the nfsdctl directory to the support/include directory, making them available for use by other components. This change is achieved without requiring any source-level #include changes due to the support/include directory being already in the default include path.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 02/16] support/export: remove unnecessary static variables in nfsd_fh expkey lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary static variables in the nfsd_fh function's CROSSMOUNT submount iteration loop. The prev and mnt variables are only needed to persist across iterations of the inner for loop, not across calls to the function. By converting them from static to local variables, the code is simplified and made more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 03/16] exportfs: remove obsolete legacy mode documentation from manpage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes outdated documentation from the exportfs manpage regarding a 'legacy mode' that was used in Linux kernels prior to version 3.1. The legacy mode, which involved injecting exports directly into the kernel using the nfsservctl() syscall, has been obsolete since then and is no longer supported by nfs-utils. The patch simplifies the description of exportfs behavior and removes references to this outdated feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 04/16] support/include: update netlink headers for all cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the netlink headers in the nfs-utils package to match the current kernel UAPI header, adding support for new cache upcalls and commands. The changes include updating the nfsd_netlink.h header to include new enum values and attribute flags, as well as adding a new sunrpc_netlink.h header for handling auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid cache upcalls via netlink. This patch is part of a larger series of updates to the nfs-utils package.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 05/16] build: add libnl3 and netlink header support for exportd and mountd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for the libnl3 library and netlink headers in the exportd and mountd builds of the nfs-utils package. It introduces new dependencies, checks for the presence of specific system headers, and updates the build configuration to use these libraries and headers when available. The goal is to enable the use of netlink messages for communication between the NFS server and clients, improving performance and functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 06/16] xlog: claim D_FAC3 as D_NETLINK
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reclaims the debugging bit D_FAC3 and assigns it a new purpose as D_NETLINK, which is used for logging about netlink activity in the xlog module. This change is made because no other part of the codebase uses the original purpose of D_FAC3. The modification involves updating the definition of D_NETLINK in the include/xlog.h file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 07/16] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based support for the svc_export cache in the nfs-utils package, allowing userspace to receive notifications of pending export requests from the kernel and respond accordingly. The patch introduces two netlink sockets: one for multicast notifications and another for command processing. If the kernel does not support the necessary genetlink family or multicast group, the patch falls back to using the existing procfs channel mechanism. The approach taken is to integrate the new netlink-based mechanism alongside the existing procfs-based mechanism, ensuring safe coexistence and allowing userspace to respond to pending export requests in a timely manner.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 08/16] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink support to the nfsd.fh cache in the exportd/mountd components of the NFS-utils package. It refactors existing code, introduces a new wrapper function for notification processing, and implements an expkey (nfsd.fh) netlink cache handler that resolves fsid-based lookups, including cross-mount submount iteration. The handler responds to requests via NFSD_CMD_EXPKEY_SET_REQS and uses the same nfsd genl family as svc_export.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 09/16] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink support to the auth.unix.ip cache in the exportd/mountd components of the nfs-utils package. It introduces a generic helper function, cache_genl_open(), which sets up the command and notification sockets for a given netlink family. The sunrpc genl family is resolved at startup and silently falls back if not supported by the kernel. The patch also adds an ip_map (auth.unix.ip) netlink cache handler that resolves IP addresses using client_resolve() and responds via SUNRPC_CMD_IP_MAP_SET_REQS with domain names or negative entries. Finally, it wires the sunrpc notification socket into several functions to enable netlink communication for the auth.unix.ip cache.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 10/16] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for the auth.unix.gid cache using netlink in the exportd/mountd components of nfs-utils. The handler resolves a pending request's UID to a group list using getpwuid() and getgrouplist(), and responds via SUNRPC_CMD_UNIX_GID_SET_REQS. This feature is only active when manage_gids is set, matching the behavior of the existing procfs handler. The patch introduces new data structures and functions for handling netlink messages and caching group IDs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 11/16] mountd/exportd: only use /proc interfaces if netlink setup fails
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the mountd/exportd code to only use /proc interfaces if netlink setup fails. Previously, both methods were attempted regardless of success or failure. The change is made because all exportd upcalls have been converted to use netlink, making the /proc interface unnecessary when netlink is available. If netlink setup fails, the code will fall back to using the /proc interfaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 12/16] support/export: check for pending requests after opening netlink sockets
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the cache_open function in the support/export/cache.c file to check for pending requests after opening netlink sockets. This ensures that any queued requests are processed before mountd comes online, preventing potential issues due to delayed request processing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 13/16] exportd/mountd: use cache type from notifications to target scanning
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the handling of netlink multicast notifications in the NFS-utils code by parsing the cache type from the notification and only issuing GET_REQS for the caches that actually need servicing, reducing unnecessary netlink round-trips. If the notification cannot be parsed, it falls back to scanning all caches for that family. The patch also updates the cache_nfsd_nl_drain function to return a mask of caches with pending requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 14/16] exportfs: add netlink support for cache flush with /proc fallback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for flushing NFS caches using netlink, which is a more modern and efficient method than the original /proc/net/rpc/*/flush write path. The new implementation tries to send SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH and NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH messages via netlink first, but falls back to the original /proc path if the sunrpc genl family cannot be resolved (which is a problem in older kernels). The patch also moves the cache_flush() function from support/nfs/cacheio.c to a new file support/export/cache_flush.c, which has access to libnl. This change allows for more flexible and efficient caching behavior in NFS exports.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils v2 15/16] exportfs: use netlink to probe kernel support, skip export_test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the `can_test` function in the exportfs utility to first try resolving the sunrpc genl family via netlink. If successful, it skips the advisory export test probe and instead relies on mountd and exportd to validate exports at cache-fill time. The patch also updates the `validate_export` function to check for this return value and skip the export test calls when netlink is available. Additionally, it links exportfs against libnl3/libnl-genl-3 and includes necessary headers for netlink support.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new command-line option and configuration setting to disable netlink support in the NFS utilities. The --no-netlink option (or -L) can be used on the command line, while the no_netlink setting can be specified in the nfs.conf file. This allows users to force the use of /proc interfaces instead of netlink, which is useful for debugging, testing, or working around kernel netlink issues.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 13/13] nfsd: add NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 13/13] nfsd: add NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 00/13] nfsd/sunrpc: add support for netlink upcalls for mountd/exportd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for netlink upcalls to the NFS daemon (nfsd) and sunrpc cache mechanism, replacing antiquated interfaces in /proc. The new netlink-based interfaces allow for easier extension of export options and better container compatibility. A companion userland patch series is also included to implement these changes in mountd/exportd and exportfs.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the removal of the `struct nfsd_genl_rqstp` definition from `nfsd.h`. They have readded it to `nfsctl.c` and restored the original definition in `nfsd.h`, making no changes to the patch.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the naming consistency of sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() and its replacement, sunrpc_cache_upcall(). The author confirmed that they will rename all occurrences of the former to the latter in v2.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the naming of sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout(), which was renamed to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn() due to its purpose being unrelated to timeouts, but rather to warn if there are no listeners.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the naming of the upcall function, explaining that it was renamed to cache_do_upcall() in anticipation of the addition of a netlink-based upcall mechanism.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the timing of netlink notifications for pending cache requests, explaining that a later patch will add a new cache_notify operation to struct cache_detail and handle sending the notification when there is a pending cache_request.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about exposing the cache_request structure outside of cache.c by adding two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), which allow callers to count and snapshot pending upcall requests without exposing the struct. The functions take care to skip entries that no longer have CACHE_PENDING set and provide a reference on each cache_head via cache_get().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed the concern that auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family because they live in the sunrpc module. The author created a new 'sunrpc' generic netlink family with its own multicast group to support cache upcall notifications for these caches.
sentiment_signals: author addressed a specific technical concern
review_comments.raw_body:
The auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches live in the sunrpc module,
so they cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family. Create a new
"sunrpc" generic netlink family with its own "exportd" multicast
group to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident
caches.

Define a YAML spec (sunrpc_cache.yaml) with a cache-type enum
(ip_map, unix_gid), a cache-notify multicast event, and the
corresponding uapi header.

Implement sunrpc_cache_notify() in cache.c, which checks for
listeners on the exportd multicast group, builds and sends a
SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY message with the cache-type attribute.

Register/unregister the sunrpc_nl_family in init_sunrpc() and
cleanup_sunrpc().

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml | 40 ++++++++++++++++++++++++
 include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h                  |  2 ++
 include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h           | 35 +++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/Makefile                           |  2 +-
 net/sunrpc/cache.c                            | 44 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/netlink.c                          | 34 +++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/netlink.h                          | 22 ++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/sunrpc_syms.c                      | 10 ++++++
 8 files changed, 188 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f4aa699598bca9ce0215bbc418d9ddcae25c0110
--- /dev/null
+++ b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
@@ -0,0 +1,40 @@
+# SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause)
+---
+name: sunrpc
+protocol: genetlink
+uapi-header: linux/sunrpc_netlink.h
+
+doc: SUNRPC cache upcall support over generic netlink.
+
+definitions:
+  -
+    type: flags
+    name: cache-type
+    entries: [ip_map, unix_gid]
+
+attribute-sets:
+  -
+    name: cache-notify
+    attributes:
+      -
+        name: cache-type
+        type: u32
+        enum: cache-type
+
+operations:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: cache-notify
+      doc: Notification that there are cache requests that need servicing
+      attribute-set: cache-notify
+      mcgrp: exportd
+      event:
+        attributes:
+          - cache-type
+
+mcast-groups:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: none
+    -
+      name: exportd
diff --git a/include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h b/include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h
index f88dc6bb17c7078781b3cf7e0371f369eddcb563..2735c332ddb736ef043f811b5e9e6ad2f57c9ce7 100644
--- a/include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h
+++ b/include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h
@@ -256,6 +256,8 @@ int sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(struct cache_detail *cd,
 				   struct cache_head **items,
 				   u64 *seqnos, int max,
 				   u64 min_seqno);
+int sunrpc_cache_notify(struct cache_detail *cd, struct cache_head *h,
+			u32 cache_type);
 
 /* Must store cache_detail in seq_file->private if using next three functions */
 extern void *cache_seq_start_rcu(struct seq_file *file, loff_t *pos);
diff --git a/include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h b/include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..6135d9b3eef155a9192d9710c8c690283ec49073
--- /dev/null
+++ b/include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h
@@ -0,0 +1,35 @@
+/* SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause) */
+/* Do not edit directly, auto-generated from: */
+/*	Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml */
+/* YNL-GEN uapi header */
+/* To regenerate run: tools/net/ynl/ynl-regen.sh */
+
+#ifndef _UAPI_LINUX_SUNRPC_NETLINK_H
+#define _UAPI_LINUX_SUNRPC_NETLINK_H
+
+#define SUNRPC_FAMILY_NAME	"sunrpc"
+#define SUNRPC_FAMILY_VERSION	1
+
+enum sunrpc_cache_type {
+	SUNRPC_CACHE_TYPE_IP_MAP = 1,
+	SUNRPC_CACHE_TYPE_UNIX_GID = 2,
+};
+
+enum {
+	SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE = 1,
+
+	__SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_MAX,
+	SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_MAX = (__SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_MAX - 1)
+};
+
+enum {
+	SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY = 1,
+
+	__SUNRPC_CMD_MAX,

 	return i;
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot);
+
+/**
+ * sunrpc_cache_notify - send a netlink notification for a cache event
+ * @cd: cache_detail for the cache
+ * @h: cache_head entry (unused, reserved for future use)
+ * @cache_type: cache type identifier (e.g. SUNRPC_CACHE_TYPE_UNIX_GID)
+ *
+ * Sends a SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY multicast message on the "exportd"
+ * group if any listeners are present. Returns 0 on success or a
+ * negative errno.
+ */
+int sunrpc_cache_notify(struct cache_detail *cd, struct cache_head *h,
+			u32 cache_type)
+{
+	struct genlmsghdr *hdr;
+	struct sk_buff *msg;
+
+	if (!genl_has_listeners(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net,
+				SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD))
+		return -ENOLINK;
+
+	msg = genlmsg_new(nla_total_size(sizeof(u32)), GFP_KERNEL);
+	if (!msg)
+		return -ENOMEM;
+
+	hdr = genlmsg_put(msg, 0, 0, &sunrpc_nl_family, 0,
+			  SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY);
+	if (!hdr) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	if (nla_put_u32(msg, SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE, cache_type)) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	genlmsg_end(msg, hdr);
+	return genlmsg_multicast_netns(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net, msg, 0,
+				       SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD, GFP_KERNEL);
+}
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(sunrpc_cache_notify);

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/13] nfsd: move struct nfsd_genl_rqstp to nfsctl.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the definition of the struct nfsd_genl_rqstp from the header file nfsd.h to the source file nfsctl.c, where it is used. This simplifies the code and reduces the risk of inconsistencies between the header and source files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/13] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall() in preparation for its future use of netlink for upcalls, if configured. The rename is applied across four files: fs/nfsd/export.c, include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h, net/sunrpc/cache.c, and net/sunrpc/svcauth_unix.c. The patch makes 7 insertions and 7 deletions to update function calls and declarations accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/13] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout() to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn(). The original function name was misleading as it did not actually handle timeouts, but instead warned if there were no listeners. This change clarifies the purpose of the function and makes its name more descriptive. The rename affects 6 files in total, with 9 insertions and 9 deletions across these files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/13] sunrpc: rename cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall() in anticipation of a future netlink-based upcall mechanism. The renaming is done to make the code more flexible and prepare it for upcoming changes. This change does not introduce any new functionality but rather sets the stage for future improvements.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/13] sunrpc: add a cache_notify callback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new callback function, cache_notify, to the struct cache_detail in the sunrpc module. The purpose of this callback is to notify when there is a pending cache_request, which will be used by a later patch that sends netlink notifications for such requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/13] sunrpc: add helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), to the Linux kernel's SunRPC implementation. These functions allow callers to count and snapshot the pending upcall request list without exposing internal data structures. The count function returns the number of requests with CACHE_PENDING set, while the snapshot function takes a reference on each item so it can be safely used after the queue lock is released. This patch will be used by the nfsd generic netlink dumpit handler for svc_export upcall requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/13] sunrpc: add a generic netlink family for cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic netlink family called 'sunrpc' to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident caches. It defines a YAML specification, implements the necessary functions in the sunrpc module, and registers/unregisters the new netlink family during initialization and cleanup. The goal is to enable cache upcalls for the auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches that live within the sunrpc module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/13] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the ip_map (auth.unix.ip) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including ip-map attribute-set and ip-map-reqs wrapper. The patch also implements two new functions: sunrpc_nl_ip_map_get_reqs_dumpit() to snapshot pending requests and send them over netlink, and sunrpc_nl_ip_map_set_reqs_doit() to parse responses from userspace and update the cache. Additionally, it wires up the ip_map_notify() callback in ip_map_cache_template to trigger SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY multicast events on cache misses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/13] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for netlink-based cache upcalls for the unix_gid (auth.unix.gid) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including a new attribute-set for unix_gids and two new operations: unix-gid-get-reqs and unix-gid-set-reqs. The patch also implements netlink-based upcalls for these operations, allowing userspace to dump pending requests and respond to them. This is done by introducing new functions that snapshot pending requests, parse responses from userspace, and update the cache accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/13] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support to the NFS daemon (nfsd) for its svc_export cache. It introduces a new netlink event, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY, which is sent when there are pending requests in the cache. The event carries information about the cache type and allows userspace to handle the notifications. The patch also includes changes to the nfsd code to wire up the notification mechanism and update the cache based on user responses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/13] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the nfsd.fh cache, allowing clients to notify the server of changes to cached file handles. The patch introduces a new 'expkey' attribute-set and operations to the nfsd YAML spec, implements netlink message handling functions, and wires up the expkey_notify callback to trigger multicast events when cache misses occur.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/13] sunrpc: add SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command that allows userspace to flush the sunrpc auth caches (ip_map and unix_gid) without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, SUNRPC_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, which specifies which caches to flush. If omitted, all sunrpc caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, with /proc fallback for older kernels.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new netlink command, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH, which allows userspace to flush the nfsd export caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, NFSD_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, that specifies which caches to flush. If omitted, all nfsd caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, and provides a fallback to /proc for older kernels.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v6 1/4] openat2: new OPENAT2_REGULAR flag support
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds support for the OPENAT2_REGULAR flag in openat2, a new system call that provides more fine-grained control over file operations. It ensures that the file system behaves correctly in these scenarios, including retrying on ENOENT errors. Additionally, it adds a check for inconsistent encryption contexts when opening files in encrypted directories.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 0/9] fuse: add io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/9] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation from the io_uring interface. The duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() is removed, decoupling request fetching from the send operation. This simplifies the control flow and reduces unnecessary parameter passing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the io-uring header copying logic in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver by moving it into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(). This makes the code more clear and centralized, allowing for better error handling and rate-limited logging. The function takes three arguments: the user-space ring buffer address, the header to be copied, and its size. It uses the copy_to_user() function to perform the actual copying and returns an error if it fails. The patch modifies several functions in the dev_uring.c file to use this new function instead of duplicating the copying logic.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 3/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module to make it more clear and centralized. It introduces a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), which handles the actual copying of data from user space to kernel space. This change improves error handling and rate-limited logging. The existing code is updated to use this new function instead of directly calling copy_from_user().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 4/9] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This simplifies both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying, particularly when kernel-managed buffer rings are added. The patch adds a new enum type `fuse_uring_header_type` with values for different header types, and modifies two inline functions `copy_header_to_ring` and `copy_header_from_ring` to use this enum type. This change will make the code more maintainable and easier to understand.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 5/9] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver, specifically for payload copying. A new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state()` is introduced to encapsulate this logic, making it more modular and easier to maintain. The existing code is simplified by removing duplicated functionality and replacing it with calls to this new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 6/9] fuse: support buffer copying for kernel addresses
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer copying in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, specifically allowing it to work directly on kernel addresses rather than requiring page extraction and kmapping. This is a preparatory step for supporting kernel-managed ring buffers in fuse-over-io-uring. The change involves modifying several functions within the FUSE codebase to handle both user-space and kernel-space buffer copying. The patch also includes some minor cleanups and warnings to ensure correct behavior when working with kernel addresses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 7/9] fuse: add io-uring kernel-managed buffer ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to use a kernel-managed buffer ring for FUSE servers communicating through the io-uring interface, reducing overhead and memory usage. The buffer ring is set on a per-queue basis and can be used incrementally, allowing non-overlapping regions of a buffer to be used simultaneously across multiple requests. This feature requires preregistration of a kernel-managed buffer ring and a memory region large enough to hold the headers before queue creation. The kernel-managed buffer ring will be pinned for the lifetime of the io-ring.

Author addressed concerns about the layout and mapping of headers in the buffer ring, explaining that they are laid out contiguously in memory and can be mapped by calculating the ent id times sizeof(struct fuse_uring_req_header). They plan to add diagrams for this and will send a v2 patch with updates, including moving the buffer rings and memory accounting infrastructure from io-uring uapi to fuse.

Author Joanne Koong responded to feedback from Bernd Schubert, stating that an additional ioctl or config setup command operation (cmd op) is not needed for the io-uring kernel-managed buffer ring capability in fuse servers. She explained that the registration flow can handle queue depth without a problem and that it simplifies the interface to have all registration sqes pass in the queue depth.

Author asked for clarification on whether the reduced queue series version could use the current registration flow, indicating uncertainty about how to proceed.
review_comments.raw_body:
I will add a visual diagram for this. The headers buffer is laid out
contiguously in memory. On the server side the server is able to map
from ent id to where in the headers buffer the data for that ent
resides by calculating ent_id * sizeof(struct fuse_uring_req_header).
I'll also add a diagram for the buffer ring as well. I am going to be
sending out a v2 that has some updates. The bulk of the fuse logic is
pretty much the same but the buffer rings and memory accounting
infrastructure will be moved to fuse instead of going through the
io-uring uapi.

---

I don't think an additional ioctl or config setup cmd op is needed
because this can go through the registration flow we currently have
without a problem. Every registration sqe will pass the queue_depth in
the fuse_uring_cmd_req data. There is logic to ensure that all ents
for a given queue set the same queue depth. For the code logic, only
the first registration sqe needs to set queue depth, but I think it
makes the interface simpler to just have all the registration sqes
pass in the queue depth, since in the dev_uring code we also account
for edge cases where the same queue gets registered concurrently on
multiple threads. I don't think a separate setup cmd op is necessary
here.

---

Do you have a link to the reduced queue series version you're
referencing? I'm not sure without looking at that code whether that
could use the current registration flow in the same way or whether
that needs a new config sqe.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 8/9] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy data transfer over io-uring for the FUSE file system. It allows privileged servers to preregister a kernel-managed buffer ring, a registered memory region, and a sparse buffer corresponding to the queue depth. This enables the elimination of memory copies between the kernel and userspace for read/write operations, resulting in improved performance. Benchmarks show approximately 20-25% increases in throughput for certain workloads. The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to implement this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 9/9] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how to set up kernel-managed buffer rings, which reduce memory allocation requirements and eliminate overheads associated with user page pinning and translation. It also describes the high-level process of using these buffer rings in FUSE operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer ring and zero-copy capabilities to the FUSE file system over io-uring, reducing memory overhead and enabling direct kernel-to-userspace operations. The changes include using registered memory regions for headers, acquiring folio references for pages, and implementing incremental buffer consumption. Benchmarks show a 20-25% increase in throughput for read-heavy operations with zero-copy enabled. The patch series has dependencies on io-uring kernel-managed buffer rings and registered bvec buffers changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, `folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc`, which allocates list_lru heads for folio objects under the control of memory cgroups (memcgs). The function is designed to be lightweight and RCU-safe, allowing it to quickly check if the list_lru heads already exist for a given memcg. If they do not exist, it allocates them. This patch is part of a larger effort to switch the THP shrinker to use list_lru, which requires this helper function to manage folio objects correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) pointed out that the patch does not address the issue of lock ordering violations between the per-vswap spinlock and the folio lock in vswap_free(), which can occur when reclaiming pages from a cgroup. He noted that this is a critical problem that needs to be fixed before the patch can be merged.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/18/26 20:53, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---

Was just hitting sent on a reply on v2 when I spotted this in my inbox.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.summary: The author addressed Lorenzo's concerns about the deferred split queue's handling of cgroups and its interaction with other shrinkers. The author acknowledged that the current implementation is suboptimal, particularly in how it sets up per-memcg queues and handles node-awareness. They explained that the list_lru API was initially designed for slab objects but has since been extended to allow explicit memcg and NUMA node selection. The author also clarified the callside locking of list_lru structures. Lorenzo's understanding of the current implementation is correct, and the author agreed that it needs improvement.

The list_lru API was initially explicitly for *slab objects*, doing
virt_to_page() and mem_cgroup_from_slab_obj() on the passed in list
heads to derive the proper queue. It was only extended more recently
in 0a97c01cd20b ("list_lru: allow explicit memcg and NUMA node
selection") to allow passing in the queue routing information and
embedding the list heads in other things (such as folios).

The only missing piece now was the support for callside locking of the
list_lru structures.

The reason it locks the list_lru first is because there can be a race
between the shrinker and a synchronous split attempt.

This is what that comment above it is about:

	/* Prevent deferred_split_scan() touching ->_refcount */

If the shrinker fails to "get" the folio while holding the shrinker
lock, it thinks it beat the free path to the list_lru lock and will
feel responsible for cleaning up the deferred split state:

/* caller holds the list_lru lock already */
static enum lru_status deferred_split_isolate(struct list_head *item,
					      struct list_lru_one *lru,
					      void *cb_arg)
{
	struct folio *folio = container_of(item, struct folio, _deferred_list);
	struct list_head *freeable = cb_arg;

	if (folio_try_get(folio)) {
		list_lru_isolate_move(lru, item, freeable);
		return LRU_REMOVED;
	}

	/* We lost race with folio_put() */
	list_lru_isolate(lru, item);
	if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
		folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
		mod_mthp_stat(folio_order(folio),
			      MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
	}
	return LRU_REMOVED;
}

So if the split path freezes before acquiring the list_lru lock, we
get them both stepping on the deferred split state.

This may or may not be safe given the current manifestation of
list_lru_del() and the folio_test_partially_mapped() tests. But it
feels pretty hairy to let them race and rely on individual tests into
what should be a coherent aggregate state.

review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the reproducible panic on s390 in the dump kernel when running NVMe standalone dump (ngdump). He stated that he would be surprised if it mattered and suggested considering it from a code clarity point of view, implying that the issue is not critical but rather an aesthetic one. No fix or revision plan was mentioned.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 03:51:07PM +0800, Kairui Song wrote:
> On Thu, Mar 19, 2026 at 4:05\u202fAM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

> > +               if (!folio)

> > +               if (mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, vma->vm_mm, gfp, entry)) {
> >                         count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_SWPIN_FALLBACK_CHARGE);
> >                         folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto next;
> >                 }
> > +               if (folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, gfp)) {
> > +                       folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto fallback;
> > +               }
> 
> Hi Johannes,
> 
> Haven't checked every detail yet, but one question here, might be
> trivial, will it be better if we fallback to the next order instead of
> fallback to 0 order directly? Suppose this is a 2M allocation and 1M
> fallback is allowed, releasing that folio and fallback to 1M will free
> 1M memory which would be enough for the list lru metadata to be
> allocated.

I would be surprised if that mattered. If we can get a 2M folio but
fail a couple of small slab requests, there is probably such extreme
levels of concurrency and pressure on the freelists that the fault has
a good chance of failing altogether and OOMing.

And if it doesn't matter, then let's consider it from a code clarity
point of view. For folio allocation and charging, we reduce the size
to try again. But the list_lru allocation is always the same size - it
would look weird to just try again on failure. If we do so based on
the logic you lay out above, now it needs a comment too...

I think there is an issue with how we call __list_lru_init(). The
existing callsites had their own memcg_kmem_online() guards. But the
THP one does not, so we're creating a memcg-aware list_lru, but the
do-while hierarchy walk in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() runs into a NULL
memcg.

Can you try the below on top of that -next checkout?

diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c

--- a/mm/list_lru.c
+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
@@ -637,7 +637,7 @@ int __list_lru_init(struct list_lru *lru, bool memcg_aware, struct shrinker *shr
 	else

+	if (mem_cgroup_disabled() || mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
 		memcg_aware = false;

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection, leading to splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. They suggested referencing shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects -> deferred_split_scan() for clarity and proposed exposing deferred_split_lru as a getter or making it static in mm/huge_memory.c.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Lorenzo's concern about the deferred split queue handling cgroups in a suboptimal fashion, specifically that it's not node-aware and leads to erratic behavior when large pages are split across nodes. The author explained that list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines reclaim walks, and confirmed that converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru would address these issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Lorenzo,

Thanks for taking an in-depth look at this!

Apologies for the late reply, I was traveling last week.

On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 01:48:06PM +0000, Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> > queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> > on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> > can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> >
> > 	alloc/unmap
> > 	  deferred_split_folio()
> > 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> > 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> So here it's:
> 
> __do_huge_pmd_anonymous_page() / do_huge_zero_wp_pmd()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_pf()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_nopf()
> -> deferred_split_folio()
> -> set_shrinker_bit()
> 
> Yeah it makes sense to make the first bit succinct anyway :)

Yep I was trying to keep the focus on those parts that interact with
the other stacks listed below. Not biblically accurate
deferred_split_folio() callsites ;)

> > 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> > 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> > 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> > 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> > 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> Hmm there's no such function is this kind of pseudocode, essentially?
> 
> Wouldn't it be clearer to reference:
> 
> shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects ->
> deferred_split_scan()?
> 
> Though I get that it adds verbosity :)
> 
> Sorry not (just) being pedantic here, also so I can understand myself :)

Yes, it's pseudocode. Direct reclaim (shrink_zones) and kswapd
(wake_all_kswapds) do the "for all nodes" loops. Nested within them
are the loops that iterate the cgroup tree (shrink_node_memcgs,
shrink_many, depending on LRU vs MGLRU). And then nested within
*those* we have the shrink_slab() calls.

Again, I tried to keep the boiler plate low and to the point. The most
important thing is: 1) the shrinker bit gets set for each node, memcg
pair that has objects, 2) shrinker visits all nodes x memcgs with it
set 3) but the shrinker scans happen on per-memcg queue, not node-aware.

Let me know if that works as is, or if you would like me to rephrase
that. I would like to keep it as simple as possible, but no simpler
than that ;)

> > 	          deferred_split_scan()
> > 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> Ok so overall there's a per-memcg memcg->split_queue, but we set a bit in
> memcg->nodeinfo[nid]->shrinker_info->unit[blah]->map for it, and when we
> enter shrink_slab_memcg(), we figure out the shrink_control from the
> for_each_set_bit() across memcg->...->unit->map?
> 
> > The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> > has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> > by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> So at this point, regardless of node, we are invoking deferred_split_scan()
> and it's the same memcg->split_queue we are walking, regardless of node?
> 
> Do let me know if my understanding is correct here!
> 
> Hmm that does sound sub-optimal.

That is exactly what's happening. We have all this dance to be precise
about the shrinker bit, but the queue itself is not node-aware.

That makes for some pretty erratic behavior.

> > list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> > streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> > widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> > queue as well.
> 
> It's odd that it wasn't used before?

The list_lru API was initially explicitly for *slab objects*, doing
virt_to_page() and mem_cgroup_from_slab_obj() on the passed in list
heads to derive the proper queue. It was only extended more recently
in 0a97c01cd20b ("list_lru: allow explicit memcg and NUMA node
selection") to allow passing in the queue routing information and
embedding the list heads in other things (such as folios).

The only missing piece now was the support for callside locking of the
list_lru structures.

The reason it locks the list_lru first is because there can be a race
between the shrinker and a synchronous split attempt.

This is what that comment above it is about:

	/* Prevent deferred_split_scan() touching ->_refcount */

If the shrinker fails to "get" the folio while holding the shrinker
lock, it thinks it beat the free path to the list_lru lock and will
feel responsible for cleaning up the deferred split state:

/* caller holds the list_lru lock already */
static enum lru_status deferred_split_isolate(struct list_head *item,
					      struct list_lru_one *lru,
					      void *cb_arg)
{
	struct folio *folio = container_of(item, struct folio, _deferred_list);
	struct list_head *freeable = cb_arg;

	if (folio_try_get(folio)) {
		list_lru_isolate_move(lru, item, freeable);
		return LRU_REMOVED;
	}

	/* We lost race with folio_put() */
	list_lru_isolate(lru, item);
	if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
		folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
		mod_mthp_stat(folio_order(folio),
			      MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
	}
	return LRU_REMOVED;
}

So if the split path freezes before acquiring the list_lru lock, we
get them both stepping on the deferred split state.

This may or may not be safe given the current manifestation of
list_lru_del() and the folio_test_partially_mapped() tests. But it
feels pretty hairy to let them race and rely on individual tests into
what should be a coherent aggregate state.

> Hi Johannes,
> 
> Haven't checked every detail yet, but one question here, might be
> trivial, will it be better if we fallback to the next order instead of
> fallback to 0 order directly? Suppose this is a 2M allocation and 1M
> fallback is allowed, releasing that folio and fallback to 1M will free
> 1M memory which would be enough for the list lru metadata to be
> allocated.

I would be surprised if that mattered. If we can get a 2M folio but
fail a couple of small slab requests, there is probably such extreme
levels of concurrency and pressure on the freelists that the fault has
a good chance of failing altogether and OOMing.

And if it doesn't matter, then let's consider it from a code clarity
point of view. For folio allocation and charging, we reduce the size
to try again. But the list_lru allocation is always the same size - it
would look weird to just try again on failure. If we do so based on
the logic you lay out above, now it needs a comment too...

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup but not their intersection, leading to potential issues when splitting large pages on other nodes during reclaim. They suggested converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists, streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers.

On 18-Mar-26 20:53, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. 
> The queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That 
> means on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering 
> reclaim can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> alloc/unmap deferred_split_folio() list_add_tail(memcg-
> >split_queue) set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes) mem_cgroup_iter() 
> shrink_slab(node, memcg) shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg) if 
> test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id) 
> deferred_split_scan() walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the
> cgroup has a single large page on the node of interest, all large
> pages owned by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be
> split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus,
> it streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's 
> used widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred 
> split queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the 
> first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling 
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages 
> are created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, 
> so the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> --- include/

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the initialization of list_lru structures in THP shrinker, acknowledged that the existing callsites had their own memcg_kmem_online() guards, and proposed a fix by adding a guard to __list_lru_init().
review_comments.raw_body:
Can you verify whether the kdump kernel boots with
cgroup_disable=memory?

I think there is an issue with how we call __list_lru_init(). The
existing callsites had their own memcg_kmem_online() guards. But the
THP one does not, so we're creating a memcg-aware list_lru, but the
do-while hierarchy walk in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() runs into a NULL
memcg.

Can you try the below on top of that -next checkout?

diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
index 1ccdd45b1d14..7c7024e33653 100644
--- a/mm/list_lru.c
+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
@@ -637,7 +637,7 @@ int __list_lru_init(struct list_lru *lru, bool memcg_aware, struct shrinker *shr
 	else
 		lru->shrinker_id = -1;
 
-	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
+	if (mem_cgroup_disabled() || mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
 		memcg_aware = false;
 #endif

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/percpu, memcontrol: Per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory accounting system to provide per-memcg-lruvec and per-node statistics for percpu allocations. It converts MEMCG_PERCPU_B from a memcg_stat_item to a node_stat_item, allowing for separate tracking of per-node and per-memcg-lruvec statistics. The patch also exposes mod_memcg_lruvec_state to enable external access to these statistics. Additionally, it simplifies the accounting of object pointers by not charging them against any specific lruvec, instead treating them as part of the pcpu objects' size.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch could have better motivated the need for per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting, but explained their intent to provide visibility into node placement within each memcg and benefit from orthogonal per-node breakdowns of percpu memory.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a weakness in patch motivation
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Michal,

Thank you for reviewing my patch! I hope you are doing well.

You're right, I could have done a better job of motivating the patch.
My intent with this patch is to give some more visibility into where
memory is physically, once you know which memcg it is in.

Percpu memory could probably be seen as "trivial" when it comes to figuring
out what node it is on, but I'm hoping to make similar transitions to the
rest of enum memcg_stat_item as well (you can see my work for the zswap
stats in [1]).

When all of the memory is moved from being tracked per-memcg to per-lruvec,
then the final vision would be able to attribute node placement within
each memcg, which can help with diagnosing things like asymmetric node
pressure within a memcg, which is currently only partially accurate.

Getting per-node breakdowns of percpu memory orthogonal to memcgs also
seems like a win to me. While unlikely, I think that we can benefit from
some amount of visibility into whether percpu allocations are happening
equally across all CPUs.

What do you think? Thank you again, I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260311195153.4013476-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/

review_comments.author: Michal Hocko
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Michal Hocko requested that the author provide a clear explanation of the reasoning behind the changes, emphasizing that understanding the 'why' is more important than understanding the 'how'.

Reviewer Michal Hocko requested a detailed explanation of why per-node statistics are needed, what benefits they bring, and an evaluation of potential issues before committing to code changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please keep in mind that WHY is very often much more important than HOW
in the patch so you should always start with the intention and
justification.

---

I think that you should have started with this intended outcome first
rather than slicing it in pieces. Why do we want to shift to per-node
stats for other/all counters? What is the cost associated comparing to the
existing accounting (if any)? Please go into details on how do you plan
to use the data before we commit into a lot of code churn.

TBH I do not see any fundamental reasons why this would be impossible
but I am not really sure this is worth the work and I also do not see
potential subtle issues that we might stumble over when getting there.
So I would appreciate if you could have a look into that deeper and
provide us with evaluation on how do you want to achieve your end goal
and what can we expect on the way. It is, of course, impossible to see
all potential problems without starting implementing the thing but a
high level evaluation would be really helpful.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged that memory in enum memcg_stat_item had no physical association, which led to a lack of visibility into node-level statistics for users. He explained his original motivation was to make memory limits tier-aware, but realized it's more about providing node-level perspective along with memcg association.

Author acknowledged a potential performance concern about the patch's impact on multi-NUMA systems, promised to gather concrete data to support their hypothesis, and will provide an update.

Author acknowledges potential issues with locking semantics and socket memory tracking, plans to test and provide numbers for confidence. Also considers MEMCG_SWAP as a special case.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for the feedback, Michal. Let me break down your questions so I
can address them one-by-one:

---

Yup, ack here as well. Here is a bit more context on why I stumbled on this
in the first place. As you are aware, I'm also working on another series
whose goal is to make memory limits tier-aware [2]. While working on this,
I realized that memory in the enum memcg_stat_item had no physical
association, which meant that identifying (1) which node / tier they were on,
and (2) which node / tier the memory should be migrated to was completely
invisible.

That was the original motivation. Looking deeper I found that this is not
even a tier problem but rather just a lack of visibility into node-level
statistics for the user.

As another example, recently I have seen an example of socket memory
landing in CXL, which is really quite strange. (Was it demoted? Was it
through a fallback allocation?) It was only visible after there was an OOM
and I could use the vmcore to inspect the data manually and figure out
the page placement.

I was thinking that it would be very nice to have this level of node-level
perspective along with the memcg association because IMO something like
this has more value in being analyzed at runtime, rather than during a
post-mortem with the vmcore, and there is more we can do by understanding
what was happening at the system when this strange placement happened.

---

For percpu specifically, I think the cost is minimal. Thankfully these
changes also have minimal effects on single-NUMA machines as well.
But let me get some concrete numbers and get back to you so that I can
back these hypotheses up.

---

Great to hear that you think this is not impossible ; -)

Yes, I also definitely see that there can be some subtle issues. One thing
I'm trying to be very mindful of is locking semantics, whether we are
introducing any new bottlenecks for updates. I'll do some testing and
come back with numbers, hopefully that can instill some more confidence
with the side effects of these patches.

As a note of concern I do believe that socket memory will be tough
to track accurately since it uses a different model of memory accounting.
I hope that there can be some steps to make it more accurate without
introducing overhead in the socket hotpaths, since those are highly
performance-sensitive.

Another concern is what to do with MEMCG_SWAP, which is not really able
to be associated with a node. But swap is unique in that it genuinely
does not take up the memory in memory. So maybe at the end of all of this
when there is only MEMCG_SWAP in memcg_stat_item, we can treat it as a
single special case. 

Thank you for your thoughts Michal, I greatly appreciate them.
I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch does not account for the additional memory overhead of moving statistics from per-memcg to per-lruvec, and requested consideration of this extra cost.
review_comments.raw_body:
You should probably also speak to extra memory overhead to move all
these stats from per-memcg to per-lruvec.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed concerns about the memory overhead of NR_PERCPU_B and lruvec_stats_percpu, providing calculations to estimate the impact on large systems.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Yosry,

Thank you for your feedback!

Here are the things that I cna see from my end:
- NR_PERCPU_B adds a byte per-node, per-cpu. I think this is manageable.
- lruvec_stats_percpu grows by 1 long in 2 arrays (state, state_prev) since
  NR_MEMCG_NODE_STAT_ITEMS grows by 1 from ~30. This is +16 bytes per
  cgroup x node x CPU. Even still, I'm not sure this is too concerning,
  on a host with 300 CPUs across 2 nodes with 100 cgroups (theoretical)
  we would see a 16 * 300 * 2 * 100 = 937 kB change, less than a mB (and
  I think this would be considered a big machine).

What do you think? Do these numbers look acceptable?

Thanks again for your insights, I hope you have a great day : -)
Joshua

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch description lacks context about the tradeoffs involved in converting MEMCG_PERCPU_B from a memcg_stat_item to a memcg_node_stat_item, suggesting that additional information is necessary for reviewers to make an informed decision.
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh I wasn't trying to say whether this is acceptable or not, just that
this is a relevant context that should be included to help people see
the tradeoff clearly and make a decision.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about performance differences between the original patch and the upstream kernel by running tests that showed negligible performance differences. The author agreed to include additional memory overhead in a v2 of the patch and made another iteration to distribute the obj_cgroup pointer size across CPUs.
review_comments.raw_body:
I went and ran a few tests, which seem to show rather negligible performance
differences (phew). I wrote a kernel module that does 100k percpu allocations
via __alloc_percpu_gfp with GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_ACCOUNT in a cgroup. I then
measured how long each allocation takes across two trials, one where I do
all 100k allocations and then free all of them at once, and another where I
interleave the allocs and frees. Everything below is ns / alloc, and the
+/- is the standard deviation across 20 trials.

+-------------+----------------+--------------+--------------+
|    Test     | linus-upstream |    patch     |     diff     |
+-------------+----------------+--------------+--------------+
| Batched     | 6586 +/- 51    | 6595 +/- 35  | +9 (0.13%)   |
| Interleaved | 1053 +/- 126   | 1085 +/- 113 | +32 (+0.85%) |
+-------------+----------------+--------------+--------------+

I'll include this, as well as the additional memory overhead that Yosry
suggested to include in a v2. I think we can get more accurate accounting
by distributing the obj_cgroup pointer size across the CPUs, so I've
gone ahead and made another iteration.

Thank you again for your insight, Michal!
Joshua

  on a host with 300 CPUs across 2 nodes with 100 cgroups (theoretical)
  we would see a 16 * 300 * 2 * 100 = 937 kB change, less than a mB (and
  I think this would be considered a big machine).

What do you think? Do these numbers look acceptable?

Thanks again for your insights, I hope you have a great day : -)
Joshua

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that the patch's motivation and tradeoffs are unclear, suggesting that additional context is necessary for reviewers to understand the design
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh I wasn't trying to say whether this is acceptable or not, just that
this is a relevant context that should be included to help people see
the tradeoff clearly and make a decision.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about performance impact by running tests that showed negligible differences between the original patch and his revised version, which includes distributing the obj_cgroup pointer size across CPUs.
review_comments.raw_body:
I went and ran a few tests, which seem to show rather negligible performance
differences (phew). I wrote a kernel module that does 100k percpu allocations
via __alloc_percpu_gfp with GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_ACCOUNT in a cgroup. I then
measured how long each allocation takes across two trials, one where I do
all 100k allocations and then free all of them at once, and another where I
interleave the allocs and frees. Everything below is ns / alloc, and the
+/- is the standard deviation across 20 trials.

+-------------+----------------+--------------+--------------+
|    Test     | linus-upstream |    patch     |     diff     |
+-------------+----------------+--------------+--------------+
| Batched     | 6586 +/- 51    | 6595 +/- 35  | +9 (0.13%)   |
| Interleaved | 1053 +/- 126   | 1085 +/- 113 | +32 (+0.85%) |
+-------------+----------------+--------------+--------------+

I'll include this, as well as the additional memory overhead that Yosry
suggested to include in a v2. I think we can get more accurate accounting
by distributing the obj_cgroup pointer size across the CPUs, so I've
gone ahead and made another iteration.

Thank you again for your insight, Michal!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau
review_comments.summary: Raised concern about the lack of explanation on what happens if the feature is not supported, suggesting it should be made more explicit in the changelog.

review_comments.author: Chao Gao
review_comments.summary: Provided an explanation for the case where the feature is not supported, and suggested making it more explicit in the changelog.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 30, 2026 at 01:07:27PM +0000, Kiryl Shutsemau wrote:
>On Thu, Mar 26, 2026 at 01:44:08AM -0700, Chao Gao wrote:
>> +	if (tdx_sysinfo.features.tdx_features0 & TDX_FEATURES0_UPDATE_COMPAT)
>> +		args.rcx |= TDX_SYS_SHUTDOWN_AVOID_COMPAT_SENSITIVE;
>
>I think you need to explain what would happen if the feature is not
>supported.

I included this explanation in the changelog:

  When the "avoid update-sensitive" feature isn't supported by the TDX
  module, proceed with updates and let userspace update at their own risk.
  ...
  
Do you mean making it more explicit:

  When the "avoid update-sensitive" feature isn't supported, proceed with
  updates. If a race occurs between module update and update-sensitive
  operations, failures happen at a later stage (e.g., incorrect TD
  measurements in attestation reports for TD build). Effectively, this
  means "let userspace update at their own risk." ...
